tldr: WebAIM provides free accessibility resources and the WAVE checker, while Assistiv Labs is a paid cloud service for remote screen reader testing on NVDA, JAWS, and VoiceOver. The move shifts you from static checks to live assistive-tech QA without local VM setup.
why_switch: Cloud access to NVDA, JAWS, and VoiceOver
No local VMs or hardware to maintain
Faster QA loops for distributed teams
Real screen-reader behavior, not just static rules
Better fit for ARIA regression testing
what_to_export: List of flows that need screen-reader testing
Existing screen-reader test scripts or steps
Known a11y issues found via WAVE
Browser and OS combinations under test
export_method: WebAIM does not host your data, so there is nothing to pull from an account. Instead, gather the artifacts your team built around WebAIM: WCAG checklists, audit spreadsheets, WAVE screenshots, and any manual screen-reader steps you wrote up. Add a column or section that flags which checks involve a screen reader, since those are the ones moving to Assistiv Labs. If you maintain a list of supported AT combinations, capture it now so it can drive your initial Assistiv Labs test matrix.
import_method: Sign up for Assistiv Labs and invite the QA and accessibility engineers who will run sessions. Pick one critical flow, such as login or checkout, and reproduce your existing screen-reader steps inside Assistiv Labs against NVDA, JAWS, and VoiceOver. Compare results with what you previously validated using WebAIM resources, and capture any new issues in your tracker. Build a small library of saved test scripts mapping your old manual steps to Assistiv Labs sessions. Train the team on streaming behavior and bandwidth so they know what to expect.
gotchas: Assistiv Labs is a paid subscription, so per-seat costs scale with QA team size
Streaming latency can affect timing-sensitive screen-reader behavior
Cloud screen readers do not fully replace testing with real assistive-tech users
WebAIM-based static checks still need a separate tool, Assistiv Labs is not a full a11y suite
Tester onboarding takes time, screen-reader keyboard shortcuts vary by AT

email.subject: Adding Assistiv Labs for remote screen-reader QA over the next two weeks
email.body:
Hi team,

We are adopting Assistiv Labs over the next 14 days to handle remote screen-reader testing on NVDA, JAWS, and VoiceOver. WebAIM resources will stay as reference material, but live AT QA will run through Assistiv Labs instead of local setups.

Plan:
- Days 1-3: provision Assistiv Labs seats for QA and a11y engineers.
- Days 4-7: pick two priority flows and reproduce existing screen-reader steps in Assistiv Labs.
- Days 8-11: build a small library of saved scripts covering NVDA, JAWS, and VoiceOver.
- Days 12-14: document the workflow, retire ad-hoc local AT setups for these flows, and share a short internal guide.

We will reassess seat count after the first month based on actual usage.

Thanks,
Your customer
